CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with clear method description (L1-norm filter ranking, one-shot vs iterative pruning) and thorough related work. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and adequate, and figures/tables evaluating FLOPs, accuracy, and wall-clock time are effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, though as a theory-minded reader I would have liked slightly more formal framing of assumptions and clearer signposting when moving between CIFAR-10 and ImageNet results. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with a clear, practical pruning algorithm and retraining strategies. Notation and method description are consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables supporting FLOP and wall-clock reductions appear informative. Writing quality is good and related work coverage thorough, though discussions of layer sensitivity in ResNets and the practical implications (e.g., skipped layers, iterative vs one-shot trade-offs) could be clarified further for easier reproduction. As a systems-oriented reader I found the presentation strong but with minor areas that would benefit from more explicit guidance for practitioners (e.g., code or pseudocode).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with clear method description (L1-norm filter ranking, one-shot vs iterative pruning) and thorough related work. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and adequate, and figures/tables evaluating FLOPs, accuracy, and wall-clock time are effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, though as a theory-minded reader I would have liked slightly more formal framing of assumptions and clearer signposting when moving between CIFAR-10 and ImageNet results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with a clear, practical pruning algorithm and retraining strategies. Notation and method description are consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables supporting FLOP and wall-clock reductions appear informative. Writing quality is good and related work coverage thorough, though discussions of layer sensitivity in ResNets and the practical implications (e.g., skipped layers, iterative vs one-shot trade-offs) could be clarified further for easier reproduction. As a systems-oriented reader I found the presentation strong but with minor areas that would benefit from more explicit guidance for practitioners (e.g., code or pseudocode).

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a simple, practical, and well-executed structured pruning method (L1 filter norm with one-shot prune-and-retrain) that yields useful empirical gains for practitioners, but is not a theoretical breakthrough. Given the work’s clear empirical value and engineering utility yet limited novelty beyond extending magnitude-pruning intuition to filters and lacking principled guarantees, I assign a moderate originality score of 3. This balances its practical contribution against its incremental nature.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a simple L1-norm filter-magnitude criterion to structured (filter-level) pruning and emphasizes a one-shot pruning + retrain protocol plus stage-wise sensitivity analysis. This is a pragmatic and well-executed empirical contribution that differentiates itself from activation-based importance and iterative schemes, but the core idea (magnitude-based filter removal) is a straightforward extension of existing magnitude-pruning intuition rather than a fundamentally new theoretical perspective. From my theory-first view the work lacks principled justification or guarantees and primarily offers empirical guidance and practical heuristics, so I judge it as a moderate (incremental) novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a simple, practical filter-pruning criterion (L1-norm of convolutional filters) and a one-shot prune-and-retrain workflow that is clearly differentiated from prior unstructured magnitude or activation-based pruning methods described in the summary. While pruning CNNs is a well-studied problem, the contribution is notable because it provides a lightweight, easily implemented structured pruning strategy that yields measurable FLOP and wall-clock gains across VGG and ResNet families and includes stage-wise analysis for residual nets. From a deployment-focused perspective this is a useful and non-trivial engineering contribution that would inform practitioners, even if it is not a radical theoretical advance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides solid empirical evidence (CIFAR-10 and ImageNet) and sensible sensitivity analyses supporting the practical claims about L1-filter pruning, but they also note missing reproducibility details. Reviewer A rightly highlights gaps in theoretical justification and some omitted strong baselines, while Reviewer B finds the experimental methodology largely sufficient and limitations acknowledged. Given the strong empirical support tempered by reproducibility and baseline/theory shortcomings, a score of 4 reflects reasonable confidence in the results while noting areas needing improvement.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence (CIFAR-10 and ImageNet) that L1-filter magnitude pruning yields structured FLOP and wall-clock reductions and includes sensible sensitivity analyses for ResNets. However, it lacks any theoretical justification or bounded guarantee for why L1-norm of filters preserves function or generalization, and no mathematical derivations are offered. The experimental methodology is reasonable but falls short of my standard: comparisons omit several strong structured-pruning and compact-architecture/knowledge-distillation baselines and code/reproducibility details are not mentioned. Overall the results are promising but important theoretical and baseline gaps leave some claims insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear, practical claims and supports them with experiments on CIFAR-10 and ILSVRC2012 showing FLOP and wall-clock reductions, comparisons to random and activation-based baselines, and sensible sensitivity analyses for ResNets. There are no formal proofs needed for the heuristic L1 filter metric and the experimental methodology includes relevant baselines and retraining protocols, though I note iterative pruning benefits are only empirically traded off against retraining cost and code availability isn’t stated. The main limitations (sensitive layers, variable effectiveness across ResNet variants) are acknowledged, so while some additional reproducibility details (public code, broader architecture variety) would strengthen the claims, the technical evidence provided largely justifies the conclusions.

main_claims: Pruning entire convolutional filters based on low weight magnitudes (L1-norm) produces structured model compression that reduces FLOPs without introducing unstructured sparsity or requiring specialized sparse libraries.
A one-shot (prune once and retrain) filter-pruning strategy is effective and much faster than layer-wise iterative pruning for many layers, and can recover accuracy with relatively short retraining.
The method achieves substantial FLOP reductions (about 30% reported for VGG on CIFAR-10 and deep ResNets) and corresponding wall-clock inference time savings with little loss in accuracy.
Sensitivity analysis of layers (especially in ResNets) reveals which layers/stages are robust or sensitive to pruning, and using stage-wise or skipped-layer pruning improves results.
_summary.methodology: Compute the L1-norm (sum of absolute weights) of each convolutional filter, sort and remove filters with smallest L1-norms along with their corresponding feature maps and downstream kernels, create a smaller network copying remaining weights, then retrain (one-shot or iteratively) to recover accuracy; use independent or greedy selection and stage-wise pruning for residual networks.
baselines: activation-based feature map pruning (various statistics as in Polyak & Wolf (2015))
limitations: Pruning sensitive layers can cause unrecoverable accuracy loss; such layers may need to be skipped.
Iterative prune-and-retrain can yield better recovery but requires many more epochs and is time-consuming.
Some networks (e.g., ResNet-34) are relatively more difficult to prune effectively than deeper ResNets.
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - CNN model compression / filter pruning

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a focus on optimization and theoretical guarantees for deep learning algorithms. Extensive experience publishing theory and principled analyses of model compression methods.
traits: Penalizes empirical-only contributions that lack theoretical justification or analysis for why the proposed criterion (L1-norm of filters) should preserve function or generalization; expects at least a formal argument or bounded guarantee.

Requires comparisons to at least three strong baselines (e.g., structured pruning methods, recent magnitude-based and importance-score methods, and knowledge-distillation + compact architecture baselines) with matched FLOPs and parameters.
Discounts claims supported only by CIFAR-10; expects experiments on at least one large-scale dataset (e.g., ImageNet) or a theoretical argument why small-dataset results transfer.

Penalizes papers that do not analyze worst-case or failure modes (e.g., which architectures or layers break the method) and that do not provide reproducibility details (seed, hyperparameters, full pruning schedule).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether a paper provides principled understanding and rigorous baseline comparisons; I give low scores to empirical heuristics that lack theory or broad validation.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist working on deploying efficient CNNs in production environments; expertise in model compression, latency measurements on real hardware, and reproducible engineering evaluations.
traits: Rewards simple, reproducible methods that provide substantial wall-clock speedups and FLOP reductions with minimal accuracy loss, even in the absence of formal theory.

Values clear empirical validation including wall-clock latency on realistic hardware (CPU/GPU/edge) and ablations showing retraining cost vs. iterative methods; will up-score papers that demonstrate practical speed/accuracy trade-offs.
Prefers methods that are easy to implement (no specialized sparse libraries) and scalable (one-shot pruning) and will penalize papers requiring complex pipelines or heavy retraining schedules.
Requires at least two diverse architecture families (e.g., VGG and ResNet) and prefers stage-wise analysis for residual networks to assess practical applicability.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize practical impact and reproducibility: if a method is simple, fast to run, and yields real latency/FLOP gains across architectures, I tend to give it a favorable score even without theoretical guarantees.
